name: systemctl
context: Inspect, start, stop, restart, enable, disable, and reload systemd units with systemctl
info: This skill covers the primary control surface for systemd units: `systemctl`. It handles human-oriented status views, machine-readable property inspection with `show`, unit lifecycle actions such as `start`, `stop`, `restart`, and `reload`, enablement actions such as `enable`, `disable`, `mask`, and `unmask`, and manager refresh with `daemon-reload` after unit files change on disk. Agents use this skill immediately after installing or editing unit files, when restarting services after deploys, when checking whether a unit is active or enabled, and when switching between the system manager and per-user managers via `--user`.
triggers: check if a unit is active
manage a user service with systemctl
body:
# systemctl

`systemctl` is the control plane for systemd units. It can list loaded units, show parsed properties, start or stop services, enable or disable install symlinks, mask units, and reload manager state after files change.

## Introspection vs State Changes

Use `status` when a human is reading and `show` when another tool needs stable fields.

```bash
systemctl status my-app.service
systemctl show my-app.service -p ActiveState -p SubState -p FragmentPath --value
systemctl list-unit-files --type=service
systemctl list-units --failed
```

## Lifecycle and Enablement

```bash
systemctl daemon-reload
systemctl restart my-app.service
systemctl reload-or-restart my-app.service
systemctl enable --now my-app.service
systemctl disable my-app.service
systemctl mask my-app.service
systemctl unmask my-app.service
```

## User Manager

Use `systemctl --user` for units under `~/.config/systemd/user`. This is separate from PID 1 and is the right surface for per-user agents, background sync jobs, and development helpers.

After a failed change, pair `systemctl` with [[systemd/journalctl]] and [[systemd/analyze]].

sections.title: Introspection
sections.body: `systemctl status UNIT` is optimized for humans and includes a short journal tail. `systemctl show UNIT -p Key --value` is the stable, parsable surface for automation. Use `list-units` for loaded units and `list-unit-files` when you need installed files and enablement state, including inactive units.

sections.title: Lifecycle actions
sections.body: Use `start`, `stop`, `restart`, `try-restart`, `reload`, or `reload-or-restart` depending on whether the process should already be running and whether it supports reloading. `reload` asks the service to reload its own configuration; it does not reread unit files from disk. After editing a unit file, run `daemon-reload` first.

sections.title: Enablement and masking
sections.body: `enable` and `disable` create or remove install symlinks declared in `[Install]`. `enable --now` is the common deploy shortcut. `mask` is stronger than disable because it prevents manual starts by linking the unit to `/dev/null`. Use `unmask` before re-enabling a previously blocked unit.

sections.title: User manager
sections.body: Per-user services are controlled with `systemctl --user` and load from user unit paths. They are ideal for developer tools, desktop agents, and user-scoped automation that should not run as root. See [[systemd/unit-files#load-path]] for the exact user paths and [[systemd/services]] for authoring the service file itself.

scripts.name: Inspect Unit
scripts.description: Read stable unit properties from `systemctl show` and emit JSON

SYSTEMD_UNIT.description: Unit to inspect, such as my-app.service

scripts.name: Manage Unit
scripts.description: Run common lifecycle and enablement actions, then report resulting unit state

SYSTEMD_ACTION.description: Action such as start, restart, enable, mask, or daemon-reload

SYSTEMD_NO_BLOCK.description: Do not wait for the job to finish
